hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (55 Wales Avenue) (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row, the properties retain original building features including their shared roofline, fenestration pattern, and round and segmentally arched window and door openings. 57 and 59 Wales Avenue have also retained their first storey projecting bay windows while 55 Wales Avenue has retained its projecting two-storey bay set beneath a gable with decorative woodwork. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential | Active laneway housing development

Constructed as a row, the properties retain original building features including their shared roofline, fenestration pattern, and round and segmentally arched window and door openings.

facade_description: 57 Wales Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row, the properties retain original building features including their shared roofline, fenestration pattern, and round and segmentally arched window and door openings. 57 and 59 Wales Avenue have also retained their first storey projecting bay windows while 55 Wales Avenue has retained its projecting two-storey bay set beneath a gable with decorative woodwork. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
